serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of chicken katsu curry omurice is approximately 400 g, comprising a mound of fried rice wrapped in a thin egg omelette, topped with one breaded chicken cutlet and curry sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Katsu Curry Omurice — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chicken Katsu Curry Omurice (cr_id F05100017), confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy (deep-fried breaded chicken + curry rice) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice and mixed rice-based dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021, American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken katsu curry omurice. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate component being white Japanese-style rice (GI ~64–72, University of Sydney GI Database) combined with a moderate fat and protein load from the egg omelette, breaded chicken, and curry sauce, which attenuates the glycemic response slightly below plain white rice. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 50 g carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 = 32.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 395 mg per 100 g, equivalent to approximately 1,580 mg sodium per standard 400 g serving, representing over 68% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium load from curry roux and seasoning; if consumed, request reduced sauce and avoid additional condiments such as soy sauce or salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load from white rice base and flour-based curry roux, with low dietary fibre (0.69 g per 100 g), resulting in rapid postprandial glucose elevation; estimated GI of 65 (medium) with a high glycemic load of 32.5 per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice, request a smaller portion of curry roux, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried breaded chicken cutlet contributes significant saturated fat (estimated 2.8 g per 100 g) and total fat at 9.95 g per 100 g; combined with high sodium (395 mg per 100 g) and potential trans fat from commercial curry roux, this dish presents multiple cardiovascular risk factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish due to its combination of high saturated fat from deep-frying, high sodium from curry sauce, and potential trans fat from commercial roux; if consumed occasionally, request baked or grilled chicken and reduced sauce.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; commercial curry roux may contain trans fat; potato in curry is a nightshade vegetable that some RA patients report worsens symptoms.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations such as this dish; if consumed, request baked chicken, monitor individual response to nightshade vegetables in the curry, and verify roux ingredients for trans fat content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Katsu Curry Omurice (チキンカツカレーオムライス) is a Japanese fusion comfort dish combining three popular Japanese food concepts: omurice (fried rice wrapped in a thin egg omelette), chicken katsu (panko-breaded deep-fried chicken cutlet), and Japanese curry sauce. The dish is assembled by stir-frying cooked white rice with vegetables and seasoning, wrapping it in a softly cooked egg omelette, placing a crispy deep-fried chicken cutlet on top, and ladling a thick, mildly spiced Japanese curry sauce — typically made from a commercial roux block containing flour, fat, and spices — over the entire plate. The result is a rich, hearty, and texturally complex dish offering the crunch of the katsu, the softness of the omelette, the savouriness of the fried rice, and the umami depth of the curry. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 174 kcal, 8.6 g protein, 9.95 g fat, and 12.5 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 395 mg — a high sodium load. Dietary fibre is low at 0.69 g per 100 g. The dish is clinically significant for individuals managing hypertension (very high sodium per serving), Type 2 Diabetes (high refined carbohydrate, low fibre, medium-high glycemic load), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat from deep-frying, high sodium). It is widely served in Japanese restaurants, Japanese-style cafes, and school canteens across Singapore and Southeast Asia. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten or egg allergies.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100017) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous white rice entry with documented rationale. All values are internally consistent and nutritionally plausible for this dish type.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.8 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy for deep-fried breaded chicken with curry sauce and fried rice, as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this specific entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this composite dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on white Japanese-style rice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for chicken katsu curry omurice was found in approved sources. 4. The input record incorrectly classified this dish under primary_cuisine: mamak and ethnic_tags: mamak; this has been corrected to Japanese cuisine as the dish is a Japanese fusion preparation. 5. Halal status from input dietary_tags has not been confirmed by an approved halal certification body and is therefore not set to true in dietary_flags. 6. The input GI value of 0 with confidence 0.389 from an unspecified Embedded GI Database has been replaced with the analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as required by approved source policy.
